prompt: Which of these best describes APR?
choices: Annual Percentage Rate — the yearly cost of borrowing, including interest plus most fees, expressed as a single rate.
Employee Stock Purchase Plan — buy your employer's shares at a discount through payroll deductions.
The 2022 federal law that updated retirement-account rules — Roth-401(k) RMD relief, 529-to-Roth rollovers, higher catch-up contributions, and more.
A grouping of accounts by tax treatment: pre-tax (401k, traditional IRA), Roth (Roth IRA / Roth 401k), and taxable (brokerage).
explanation: Annual Percentage Rate — the yearly cost of borrowing, including interest plus most fees, expressed as a single rate. The figure lenders must disclose under the Truth in Lending Act so you can compare loan and credit-card offers on one number. When you're earning rather than borrowing, compare APY instead — unlike APR, APY accounts for compounding.

prompt: Which of these best describes APY?
choices: Employee Stock Purchase Plan — buy your employer's shares at a discount through payroll deductions.
The 2022 federal law that updated retirement-account rules — Roth-401(k) RMD relief, 529-to-Roth rollovers, higher catch-up contributions, and more.
A grouping of accounts by tax treatment: pre-tax (401k, traditional IRA), Roth (Roth IRA / Roth 401k), and taxable (brokerage).
Annual Percentage Yield — the effective annual return after compounding is applied. Use this (not APR) when comparing savings/CD rates.
explanation: Annual Percentage Yield — the effective annual return after compounding is applied. Use APY (not APR) when comparing savings or CD rates — APY already bakes in compounding so higher-frequency compounding shows up as a higher APY.

prompt: Which of these best describes an HSA?
choices: How much of your wealth is in a single holding — high concentration = high single-stock risk.
Health Savings Account — triple-tax-advantaged account for medical expenses, only if you have an HDHP.
Restricted Stock Unit — company shares that vest to you over time.
Your typical monthly spending — anchors the FI / Coast FI calculations.
explanation: Health Savings Account — triple-tax-advantaged account for medical expenses, only if you have an HDHP. Contributions reduce taxable income, growth is tax-free, and qualified withdrawals are tax-free. Unlike an FSA, the balance rolls over forever and you keep it if you switch jobs.

prompt: Which of these best describes an FSA?
choices: The process of earning equity over time — typically 4-year vests with a 1-year cliff.
A dividend taxed at the lower long-term capital gains rate instead of as ordinary income.
An extra 0.9% Medicare tax on wages above $200k (single) / $250k (MFJ).
Flexible Spending Account — pre-tax money for medical expenses.
explanation: Flexible Spending Account — pre-tax money for medical expenses. A workplace account where you set aside pre-tax money to spend on medical costs. Use-it-or-lose-it within the year (with a small carry-over allowance).

prompt: Which of these best describes an IRA?
choices: A tax-advantaged account for retirement savings — Traditional (pre-tax) or Roth (after-tax) — with annual contribution limits.
A health insurance plan with a higher deductible but lower premiums.
A type of company equity award that delivers shares after a vesting schedule.
The annual Social Security wage cap above which FICA no longer applies.
explanation: Individual Retirement Account — a tax-advantaged account for retirement savings. Traditional IRAs give a potential tax deduction now; Roth IRAs give tax-free withdrawals later. Both have annual contribution limits (indexed for inflation), plus catch-up provisions over age 50.

prompt: Which of these best describes a 401(k)?
choices: Safe Withdrawal Rate — the percentage of a portfolio that can be withdrawn annually without depleting it.
A workplace retirement plan you contribute to from your paycheck.
The IRS rule that sets minimum withdrawals from pre-tax retirement accounts after a certain age.
An account funded with after-tax dollars whose withdrawals in retirement are tax-free.
explanation: A workplace retirement plan you contribute to from your paycheck. Traditional 401(k) contributions reduce your taxable income now; Roth 401(k) contributions are after-tax but grow tax-free. Many employers add matching contributions — free money worth capturing.

prompt: Which of these best describes a Roth IRA?
choices: A tax-deductible retirement account where contributions lower your taxable income today.
An Individual Retirement Account funded with after-tax dollars — withdrawals in retirement are tax-free.
A retirement plan for self-employed individuals with higher contribution limits.
A use-it-or-lose-it account for healthcare expenses tied to a high-deductible health plan.
explanation: A Roth IRA is funded with after-tax dollars so qualified withdrawals in retirement — including all growth — are completely tax-free. Contributions (not earnings) can be withdrawn anytime without penalty. Income limits apply to direct contributions, but the backdoor Roth is a workaround for high earners.

prompt: Which of these best describes a Traditional IRA?
choices: A retirement account where contributions are after-tax and withdrawals are tax-free.
A plan that lets self-employed people save and deduct up to ~25% of net earnings.
A pre-tax retirement account — contributions may be deductible, and withdrawals in retirement are taxed as ordinary income.
A retirement account that requires minimum distributions starting at age 59½.
explanation: A Traditional IRA holds pre-tax money (if you're eligible to deduct). Contributions may reduce taxable income today; withdrawals in retirement are taxed as ordinary income. Required Minimum Distributions begin at age 73 under SECURE 2.0.

prompt: Which of these best describes an RMD?
choices: A penalty for withdrawing from a retirement account before age 59½.
Required Minimum Distribution — the IRS-mandated minimum you must withdraw each year from pre-tax retirement accounts after a certain age.
A contribution limit that applies only to employer-sponsored plans.
A tax credit for low-income savers who contribute to retirement accounts.
explanation: Required Minimum Distribution — the IRS-mandated minimum you must withdraw each year from pre-tax retirement accounts (Traditional IRA, 401(k), etc.) after age 73 under SECURE 2.0. Roth IRAs are exempt. Missing an RMD triggers a 25% penalty on the shortfall.

prompt: Which of these best describes the AMT?
choices: A 3.8% surtax on investment income for high-income filers.
Alternative Minimum Tax — a parallel tax calculation that ensures high-income filers pay at least a minimum amount.
A penalty for failing to take Required Minimum Distributions on time.
The maximum percentage a worker pays in payroll taxes.
explanation: Alternative Minimum Tax — a parallel tax calculation that ensures high-income filers pay at least a minimum amount. You pay the higher of regular tax or AMT. Common triggers include exercising Incentive Stock Options (ISOs) and large miscellaneous deductions.

prompt: Which of these best describes AGI?
choices: Adjusted Gross Income — gross income minus above-the-line deductions like IRA contributions, student loan interest, and HSA contributions.
A penalty for early retirement account withdrawals.
The total amount an employee contributes to a 401(k) before employer match.
A tax on wages that funds Social Security and Medicare.
explanation: Adjusted Gross Income — gross income minus above-the-line deductions like IRA contributions, student loan interest, and HSA contributions. AGI is the starting point for many phase-outs, tax-credit calculations, and the Roth IRA income limits.

prompt: Which of these best describes MAGI?
choices: The tax owed on a retirement distribution before any credits.
Modified Adjusted Gross Income — AGI with certain deductions added back; used to determine Roth IRA eligibility and other phase-outs.
A type of employer contribution that vests immediately.
The portion of a paycheck subject to FICA taxes.
explanation: Modified Adjusted Gross Income — AGI with certain deductions added back (e.g., IRA deduction, student-loan interest). MAGI is used to determine Roth IRA contribution limits, whether you can deduct a Traditional IRA, and IRMAA Medicare surcharges.

prompt: Which of these best describes long-term capital gains (LTCG)?
choices: Gains on assets sold within a year, taxed as ordinary income.
A dividend taxed at the lower long-term capital gains rate instead of as ordinary income.
Gains on assets held longer than one year, taxed at preferential rates (0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income).
The annual cap on 401(k) employee contributions.
explanation: Gains on assets held longer than one year are taxed at preferential long-term capital gains rates: 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on your taxable income. Most middle-income investors pay 15%. Holding an investment just past the one-year mark can significantly reduce the tax owed on the gain.

prompt: Which of these best describes your marginal tax rate?
choices: The average percentage of your total income paid in federal income tax.
The tax rate applied to the last dollar of your taxable income — the rate at which your next dollar of income will be taxed.
The flat rate that applies to all self-employment income.
The percentage of gross income withheld for FICA taxes.
explanation: Your marginal tax rate is the rate applied to the last dollar of your taxable income — the tax bracket you're currently in. It determines the value of deductions and the cost of additional income. Many people confuse it with their effective rate (lower, because lower brackets apply to lower slices of income).

prompt: Which of these best describes your effective tax rate?
choices: The tax bracket percentage that applies to your highest dollar of income.
The total federal income tax you paid divided by your total taxable income — your actual average tax rate.
The FICA rate applied to wages up to the Social Security wage base.
The rate at which interest income is taxed for AMT purposes.
explanation: Your effective tax rate is total federal income tax divided by taxable income — the actual average percentage you paid. It's always lower than your marginal rate because lower tax brackets apply to lower slices of your income. Effective rate is the right number for comparing tax burdens across people.

prompt: Which of these best describes the Safe Withdrawal Rate (SWR)?
choices: Flexible Spending Account — pre-tax money for medical expenses.
Safe Withdrawal Rate — the percentage of your portfolio you can withdraw each year and (probably) not run out.
Employee Stock Purchase Plan — buy your employer's shares at a discount through payroll deductions.
The 2022 federal law that updated retirement-account rules — RMD relief, 529-to-Roth rollovers, higher catch-up contributions.
explanation: Safe Withdrawal Rate — the percentage of your portfolio you can withdraw each year and (probably) not run out. The famous '4% rule' (Bengen / Trinity Study) says ~4% of your starting nest egg, inflation-adjusted thereafter, has historically lasted a 30-year retirement. Lower rates are more conservative.

prompt: Which of these best describes Coast FI?
choices: The short symbols used to identify stocks, ETFs, and mutual funds on an exchange.
A 401(k) for self-employed people with no employees other than a spouse.
How far your portfolio's actual mix has wandered from your target allocation as markets move.
The point where your existing investments will grow into a full retirement nest egg on their own — even if you stop contributing.
explanation: Coast FI is the point where your existing investments will grow into a full retirement nest egg on their own — even if you stop contributing. You still need to cover current expenses, but you no longer need to save for retirement. A common milestone on the way to full Financial Independence.

prompt: What does FIRE stand for in personal finance?
choices: A projection chart detail mode that draws one line for total investments.
Married Filing Separately — each spouse files their own return.
The portion of your paycheck routed into a Dependent Care FSA before tax is withheld.
Financial Independence, Retire Early — a movement around saving aggressively to reach FI well before traditional retirement age.
explanation: FIRE stands for Financial Independence, Retire Early — a movement built around aggressive saving and investing to reach financial independence well before the traditional retirement age of 65. Variants include LeanFIRE (minimal lifestyle) and FatFIRE (higher spending).

prompt: Which of these best describes Financial Independence (FI)?
choices: Financial Independence — your investments throw off enough income to cover your expenses, so work becomes optional.
A projection chart detail mode that draws one line for total investments.
IRS rule that taxes a Roth conversion in proportion to your pre-tax IRA balances.
Health Savings Account — triple-tax-advantaged account for medical expenses.
explanation: Financial Independence means your investments generate enough income (or you have enough wealth at your safe withdrawal rate) to cover your expenses — making paid work optional rather than required. The FI number is typically 25× annual expenses (the inverse of a 4% SWR).

prompt: Which of these best describes compounding frequency?
choices: The effective annual return on a savings account, including all compounding.
How often interest is calculated and added to your balance — daily, monthly, quarterly, or annually.
The minimum balance required to avoid a monthly fee.
The penalty rate charged for early CD withdrawal.
explanation: Compounding frequency is how often interest is calculated and added to your balance. The more frequently it compounds, the higher the effective yield (APY). Daily compounding is common for savings accounts; annual compounding is used in simple bond math.

prompt: Which of these best describes an HDHP?
choices: Annual Percentage Yield — the effective annual return after compounding is applied.
High-Deductible Health Plan — required to contribute to an HSA.
How the projection chart slices your investments: a single line, three lines by tax bucket, or one line per account.
The process of earning equity over time — typically 4-year vests with a 1-year cliff.
explanation: High-Deductible Health Plan — a health insurance plan with a higher deductible (you pay more out of pocket before insurance kicks in) but typically lower premiums. Being enrolled in an HDHP is the eligibility requirement to open and fund an HSA.

prompt: Which of these best describes FICA?
choices: Federal Insurance Contributions Act — the payroll tax that funds Social Security and Medicare.
Sticker-price dollars — what a number says without adjusting for inflation.
A dividend taxed at the lower long-term capital gains rate instead of as ordinary income.
An Individual Retirement Account funded with after-tax dollars.
explanation: FICA (Federal Insurance Contributions Act) is the payroll tax that funds Social Security and Medicare. Roughly 7.65% of wages: 6.2% Social Security (capped at the annual wage base) + 1.45% Medicare (uncapped). Your employer pays a matching amount; self-employed workers pay both halves (15.3%) via self-employment tax.

prompt: Which of these best describes the NIIT?
choices: A workplace retirement plan you contribute to from your paycheck.
Net Investment Income Tax — an extra 3.8% tax on investment income above ~$200k (single) / $250k (MFJ).
Alternative Minimum Tax — a parallel tax calculation that ensures high-income filers pay at least a minimum amount.
The Inputs page where you tune assumptions about long-term growth rates.
explanation: Net Investment Income Tax — an extra 3.8% tax on investment income (interest, dividends, capital gains, rental income) once your MAGI exceeds $200k (single) or $250k (married filing jointly). It applies in addition to regular income tax.

prompt: Which of these best describes an ISO?
choices: A company share that vests to you over time without requiring a purchase.
Incentive Stock Option — an employer-granted right to buy company stock at a set price, with potential AMT and capital-gains tax advantages.
A retirement plan contribution that reduces your current taxable income.
A dividend from a foreign corporation taxed at the ordinary income rate.
explanation: An Incentive Stock Option (ISO) lets you buy company stock at a fixed strike price. The spread at exercise is NOT regular income but IS an AMT preference item — a common AMT trigger. If you hold shares at least two years from grant and one year from exercise, the eventual gain qualifies for long-term capital gains rates.

prompt: Which of these best describes an RSU?
choices: An employer-granted right to buy company stock at a fixed price below market value.
A restricted stock unit — company shares that vest to you over time, taxed as ordinary income at vesting.
A plan that lets employees buy company stock at a discount through payroll deductions.
An individual retirement account funded with after-tax dollars.
explanation: A Restricted Stock Unit (RSU) is a promise of company stock that vests over time (typically 4 years with a 1-year cliff). Unlike options, RSUs have value even if the stock price stays flat. The fair market value at vesting is ordinary income; any subsequent appreciation is a capital gain.

prompt: Which of these best describes an ESPP?
choices: A plan that lets employees buy company stock at a discount through payroll deductions.
A type of restricted stock that vests automatically on a schedule.
A severance-pay arrangement funded by the employer.
A tax-advantaged account for purchasing employer-offered health plans.
explanation: Employee Stock Purchase Plan — a benefit that lets employees buy company stock (often at a 15% discount) through payroll deductions. A qualifying disposition (holding two years from offering + one year from purchase) taxes the discount as ordinary income but treats additional gains at long-term capital gains rates.

prompt: Which of these best describes vesting?
choices: The IRS rule that determines the tax treatment of employer stock options.
The process of earning equity over time — typically a 4-year schedule with a 1-year cliff.
The taxable value of benefits provided by an employer in addition to salary.
The transfer of unvested equity to a new employer after leaving a company.
explanation: Vesting is the process of earning equity over time. A common schedule: 1-year cliff (nothing until you complete year one, then 25% unlocks), then monthly/quarterly over the next 3 years until 100% is yours. Leaving before full vest means forfeiting unvested shares.

prompt: Which of these best describes cost basis?
choices: The original value of an asset for tax purposes — what you paid, which sets your eventual capital gain or loss.
The current market value of all your investments combined.
The fee charged by a brokerage to execute a trade.
The price at which a stock option can be exercised.
explanation: Cost basis is the original value of an asset for tax purposes — typically what you paid plus commissions. Your capital gain (or loss) is the sale price minus cost basis. Tracking it correctly matters especially for shares acquired at different times (FIFO, specific identification, or average cost methods).

prompt: Which of these best describes a qualified dividend?
choices: A dividend from a REIT, taxed at ordinary income rates.
A dividend taxed at the lower long-term capital gains rate (0%, 15%, or 20%) instead of as ordinary income.
A dividend that automatically reinvests into more shares.
A dividend paid by a foreign corporation that is exempt from U.S. tax.
explanation: A qualified dividend is taxed at the preferential long-term capital gains rates (0%, 15%, or 20%) rather than as ordinary income. To qualify, the dividend must be paid by a U.S. or eligible foreign corporation, and you must hold the stock for more than 60 days around the ex-dividend date.

prompt: Which of these best describes nominal dollars?
choices: Dollars adjusted for inflation to reflect today's purchasing power.
Sticker-price dollars — the face value of a number without adjusting for inflation.
The after-tax value of a dollar in a pre-tax retirement account.
A unit of currency that accounts for exchange-rate fluctuations.
explanation: Nominal dollars are sticker-price figures — the amount stated without adjusting for inflation. A salary of $100k today and $100k in 20 years are the same in nominal terms but very different in real (inflation-adjusted) purchasing power. Financial projections often show both.

prompt: Which of these best describes 'real' dollars (as in 'real return')?
choices: Dollars expressed in their actual cash value at the time of the transaction.
Dollars that have been adjusted for inflation to reflect constant purchasing power.
Dollars held in a FDIC-insured account.
Dollars converted from a foreign currency at the official exchange rate.
explanation: Real dollars (or real return) are adjusted for inflation. If your portfolio grew 7% but inflation was 3%, your real return was about 4%. Using real returns helps you think about actual increases in purchasing power, not just nominal gains.

prompt: Which of these best describes a 529 plan?
choices: A tax-advantaged account for K-12 and college expenses with a $2,000/year contribution limit.
A tax-advantaged savings plan for education expenses — contributions grow tax-free and withdrawals for qualified education expenses are tax-free.
A retirement savings account available only to public school employees.
An employer-matched savings account for apprenticeship and vocational training.
explanation: A 529 plan is a state-sponsored tax-advantaged account for education expenses. Contributions grow tax-free; qualified withdrawals (tuition, room & board, books) are tax-free. SECURE 2.0 allows unused 529 balances to be rolled into a Roth IRA (with limits), reducing the 'trapped money' risk.

prompt: Which of these best describes the early-withdrawal penalty on retirement accounts?
choices: A 50% excise tax on Required Minimum Distributions not taken on time.
A 10% additional tax on distributions taken before age 59½ from most retirement accounts.
A fee charged by a brokerage for closing a retirement account.
A penalty that applies when you exceed the annual IRA contribution limit.
explanation: Taking money out of a Traditional IRA or 401(k) before age 59½ generally triggers a 10% additional federal tax on top of the regular income tax. Notable exceptions: substantially equal periodic payments (72(t)), first-time home purchase (IRA only), higher education, disability, and others. Roth IRA contributions (not earnings) can be withdrawn anytime penalty-free.

prompt: Which of these best describes Married Filing Jointly (MFJ)?
choices: A filing status available to married couples where each spouse reports only their own income on a separate return.
A filing status for unmarried taxpayers who pay more than half the cost of maintaining a home for a qualifying person.
A filing status where both spouses combine income and deductions on one return, generally yielding lower taxes than filing separately.
A filing status that applies when a spouse dies during the tax year.
explanation: Married Filing Jointly means both spouses combine income and deductions on one return. It generally results in lower taxes than Married Filing Separately and gives access to higher phase-out thresholds for credits and deductions. Most married couples benefit from MFJ unless one spouse has significant debts or liabilities.

prompt: Which of these best describes the standard deduction?
choices: A deduction equal to the amount you donated to qualified charities.
A fixed dollar amount that reduces taxable income without needing to itemize — most taxpayers use this.
The maximum amount deductible for business expenses from a sole proprietorship.
A deduction for mortgage interest available only to homeowners.
explanation: The standard deduction is a fixed dollar amount that reduces taxable income without requiring you to list (itemize) every deduction. It is indexed annually for inflation, so the exact figure rises most years. About 90% of filers take the standard deduction because it exceeds what they could itemize.

prompt: Which of these best describes the backdoor Roth strategy?
choices: Converting a 401(k) to an IRA after leaving an employer.
A two-step workaround for high earners: contribute (non-deductible) to a Traditional IRA, then convert it to a Roth IRA.
A strategy for reducing RMDs by delaying 401(k) distributions.
Withdrawing Roth contributions early to avoid the 10% penalty.
explanation: The backdoor Roth is a two-step workaround: contribute to a Traditional IRA on a non-deductible basis, then convert the balance to a Roth IRA. High earners above the Roth IRA income limits use this to get money into a Roth. Watch out for the pro-rata rule if you have other pre-tax IRA balances.

prompt: Which of these best describes the IRS pro-rata rule for IRA conversions?
choices: A rule that limits Roth IRA contributions based on earned income.
IRS rule that taxes a Roth conversion in proportion to your pre-tax IRA balances — including Traditional, SEP, and SIMPLE IRAs.
A rule requiring equal annual distributions from inherited IRAs.
A rule limiting the deductibility of losses from passive investments.
explanation: The pro-rata rule treats all your non-Roth IRAs as one pot when you convert. The taxable fraction equals pre-tax balance divided by total IRA balance. For example, if you have $90k pre-tax and $10k non-deductible, 90% of any conversion is taxable. Rolling pre-tax IRA money into a 401(k) first can sidestep the rule.

prompt: Which of these best describes a SEP-IRA?
choices: A Simplified Employee Pension — a retirement plan for self-employed people and small businesses that allows large tax-deductible contributions.
A tax-advantaged account for medical expenses paired with a high-deductible health plan.
A type of taxable brokerage account with no contribution limits.
A government retirement plan for public-sector workers.
explanation: A SEP-IRA (Simplified Employee Pension) is a retirement plan for self-employed people and small businesses. Contributions can be up to ~25% of net self-employment earnings (subject to an annual cap that the IRS indexes) and are fully tax-deductible. SEPs are simple to open and have no fixed annual contribution requirement.

prompt: Which of these best describes a SIMPLE IRA?
choices: A savings account with a simple, fixed interest rate and no contribution limits.
A Savings Incentive Match Plan for Employees — a retirement plan for small businesses (≤100 employees) with required employer matching.
A type of IRA that requires no beneficiary designation.
An IRA that can only hold government-backed securities.
explanation: A SIMPLE IRA (Savings Incentive Match Plan for Employees) is a retirement plan for small businesses with 100 or fewer employees. Employees can contribute up to an annual limit the IRS indexes; employers must either match up to 3% of compensation or contribute a flat 2%. Early withdrawals within the first two years face a 25% penalty.

prompt: Which of these best describes a DCFSA?
choices: A grouping of accounts by tax treatment: pre-tax, Roth, and taxable.
Your projected year-over-year salary path including raises and promotions.
Dependent Care FSA — pre-tax money set aside for childcare or eldercare.
A projection chart detail mode that draws one line per investment account.
explanation: Dependent Care FSA — a workplace account that lets you contribute pre-tax dollars (subject to an annual cap) for qualified childcare or eldercare expenses, lowering your taxable income. Funds are use-it-or-lose-it within the plan year. The dependent must generally be under age 13.

prompt: A backdoor Roth IRA is subject to which IRS rule that can create unexpected taxes if you hold pre-tax IRA balances?
choices: The wash-sale rule
The step-transaction doctrine
The pro-rata rule
The 5-year aging rule
explanation: The pro-rata rule treats all your non-Roth IRAs (Traditional, SEP, SIMPLE) as one pot. The taxable fraction of a conversion equals pre-tax balance divided by total balance, so a large pre-tax balance makes a backdoor conversion partly taxable. The usual fix is rolling pre-tax IRA dollars into a 401(k) first.

prompt: At what age do Required Minimum Distributions generally begin for Traditional 401(k) and IRA balances under SECURE 2.0?
explanation: Under SECURE 2.0 (enacted 2022), Required Minimum Distributions from pre-tax retirement accounts generally begin at age 73 (rising to 75 in 2033). They do not apply to Roth IRAs (though Roth 401(k)s were subject to them until SECURE 2.0 eliminated that requirement). The first RMD may be delayed to April 1 of the following year.

prompt: Which types of income are subject to the Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT)?
choices: Only long-term capital gains from stock sales
Wages, self-employment income, and IRA distributions
Interest, dividends, capital gains, rental income, and passive activity income above the MAGI threshold
Only income from foreign investments
explanation: The 3.8% NIIT applies to the lesser of net investment income or the excess of MAGI over $200k (single) / $250k (MFJ). Net investment income includes interest, dividends, capital gains, rental income, royalties, and passive activity income. Wages, self-employment income, and distributions from IRAs and qualified plans are explicitly excluded.

prompt: When you exercise Incentive Stock Options (ISOs), which AMT adjustment triggers?
choices: The exercise spread is ordinary income for regular tax, creating no AMT difference
The exercise spread (fair market value minus strike price) is an AMT preference item — not regular income, but added back for AMT purposes
The entire sale proceeds are subject to AMT in the year of exercise
ISO exercises are always exempt from AMT for non-employees
explanation: When you exercise ISOs, the spread (FMV minus strike price) is NOT regular taxable income but IS an Alternative Minimum Tax adjustment item. It gets added to AMTI. In a high-spread year (large grants exercised near IPO) this can create a large AMT bill even though you received no cash. The standard response is careful exercise timing or selling enough shares to cover the AMT.

prompt: The Roth conversion ladder is primarily used to avoid which penalty for early retirees before age 59½?
choices: The net investment income tax (NIIT)
The 25% SIMPLE IRA early-withdrawal penalty
The 10% early-withdrawal penalty on qualified retirement plan distributions
The excess contribution penalty
explanation: Early retirees convert Traditional 401(k)/IRA balances to Roth each year, then wait five years for each converted tranche to become penalty-free. Because Roth IRA contributions (and seasoned conversions) can be withdrawn without the 10% penalty, this creates a bridge to age 59½. Execution requires five years of living expenses in non-retirement accounts as a runway.

prompt: For an ESPP qualifying disposition, what are the required holding periods?
choices: One year from purchase date only
Two years from the offering date AND one year from the purchase date
Six months from both the offering date and purchase date
No holding period — all ESPP sales are always qualifying dispositions
explanation: For a Section 423 ESPP qualifying disposition, you must hold the shares at least two years from the offering (grant) date AND one year from the purchase date. In a qualifying disposition, the discount is taxed as ordinary income (capped at the actual discount if gains are smaller), and additional appreciation is a long-term capital gain. A disqualifying disposition taxes more of the gain as ordinary income.

prompt: What happens to your direct Roth IRA contribution limit as your MAGI increases above the phase-out range?
choices: Your limit increases proportionally with income
Your limit is reduced proportionally until it phases out to zero at the top of the range
Your limit stays the same but the contribution is treated as non-deductible
You become eligible for a higher contribution limit through a catch-up provision
explanation: Direct Roth IRA contributions phase out over a MAGI range (the IRS indexes the band each year). Within the range your allowed contribution decreases proportionally. Above the top of the range, direct contributions are $0. High earners above the limit use the backdoor Roth (non-deductible Traditional IRA → conversion) as a legal workaround.

prompt: Under SECURE 2.0, unused 529 funds can be rolled into a Roth IRA. What is the lifetime rollover limit per beneficiary?
choices: No limit — the full 529 balance can be rolled over
explanation: SECURE 2.0 allows rolling unused 529 funds into the beneficiary's Roth IRA, subject to a $35,000 lifetime limit per beneficiary. The 529 account must be at least 15 years old, and annual rollovers are capped at the Roth IRA annual contribution limit (minus other Roth contributions). The beneficiary must have earned income at least equal to the amount rolled over.

prompt: What is the wash-sale rule and what does it disallow?
choices: A rule requiring wash trades to be reported separately on Schedule B
A rule that disallows a tax loss if you buy a 'substantially identical' security within 30 days before or after the sale at a loss
A rule that treats repeated day-trading losses as non-deductible hobby losses
A rule that disallows capital losses that exceed capital gains in a single year
explanation: The wash-sale rule (IRC §1091) disallows a capital loss if you purchase a 'substantially identical' security within 30 days before or after the loss sale. The disallowed loss is added to the basis of the new shares, effectively deferring rather than eliminating it. Common mistake: selling VTI at a loss and buying VOO (different but substantially identical broad-market ETFs) can still trigger the rule.

prompt: What is a 72(t) SEPP and what penalty does it avoid?
choices: A rule allowing penalty-free IRA withdrawals after age 72
Substantially Equal Periodic Payments — a series of IRA withdrawals that avoids the 10% early-withdrawal penalty if maintained for at least 5 years or until age 59½
A provision that lets retirees reduce RMDs by electing SEPP distributions
A tax credit for substantially equal pension payments received during early retirement
explanation: IRC §72(t) allows penalty-free early IRA withdrawals if you take Substantially Equal Periodic Payments (SEPP). The payments must continue for at least five years OR until you turn 59½, whichever is later. Three IRS-approved calculation methods (RMD, amortization, annuitization) determine the payment amount. Modifying or stopping the payments before the required period ends triggers the 10% penalty — retroactively on all prior distributions.

prompt: What is IRMAA and how does MAGI affect Medicare Part B and D premiums?
choices: IRMAA reduces Medicare premiums for low-income beneficiaries based on MAGI
IRMAA adds a surcharge to Medicare Part B and D premiums when MAGI exceeds certain thresholds — using two-year-old income data
IRMAA is a flat fee all Medicare beneficiaries pay regardless of income
IRMAA affects only Medicare Part A hospital coverage costs
explanation: IRMAA (Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount) adds a surcharge to Medicare Part B and Part D premiums when MAGI exceeds threshold tiers (the IRS/CMS index the brackets each year). The surcharge uses income from two years prior (e.g. this year's premiums use MAGI from two years ago). A large Roth conversion or capital gain can unexpectedly push you into a higher IRMAA tier, significantly raising Medicare costs.

prompt: Under the SECURE Act, most non-spouse beneficiaries who inherit an IRA must follow which rule?
choices: Stretch distributions over the beneficiary's remaining life expectancy
Take all funds within 5 years of the original owner's death
Empty the account within 10 years of the original owner's death
Transfer the balance to their own IRA without any distribution requirement
explanation: The SECURE Act (2019) eliminated the stretch IRA for most non-spouse beneficiaries, replacing it with a 10-year rule: the entire inherited IRA balance must be distributed by the end of the 10th year following the owner's death. If the original owner had begun RMDs, the beneficiary must also take annual distributions during the 10 years (per IRS proposed regulations). Exceptions exist for eligible designated beneficiaries (spouses, minor children, disabled individuals, etc.).
source: SECURE Act of 2019; IRS Proposed Regs (Feb 2022)

prompt: At what approximate 2024 taxable income threshold does the 0% long-term capital gains rate end for a single filer?
explanation: For single filers in 2024, the 0% LTCG rate applies to taxable income up to $47,025 (after standard deduction). Above that threshold to $518,900, the rate is 15%; above $518,900 it's 20%. This means someone in a lower income year (e.g., early retirement before Social Security) may be able to harvest significant capital gains completely tax-free — a strategy called zero-rate harvesting.

prompt: The Roth IRA 5-year rule has TWO separate applications. Which statement correctly describes one of them?
choices: Roth IRA contributions must be held 5 years before they can be withdrawn penalty-free
The 5-year clock for a Roth IRA starts on January 1 of the year of the first conversion, not the first contribution
Each Roth conversion has its own separate 5-year clock for penalty-free withdrawal of converted amounts before age 59½
Each Roth IRA account has its own independent 5-year clock for determining qualified distributions
explanation: There are two Roth 5-year clocks: (1) The earnings qualification clock — starts January 1 of the first year you contributed to ANY Roth IRA, which is universal across all your Roth IRAs. (2) The conversion clock — each conversion has its own 5-year holding period for penalty-free withdrawal of the converted amount before 59½. When you roll a Roth 401(k) into a Roth IRA, the rollover inherits the IRA's existing clock for earnings purposes, but conversions made during the rollover can start a new conversion clock.

prompt: For a self-employed person with a Solo 401(k), which of these best describes the combined contribution limit?
choices: Employee deferral only, capped at the standard 401(k) limit
Employer profit-sharing contribution only, up to 25% of W-2 wages
Employee deferral PLUS employer profit-sharing (up to 25% of net self-employment income), combined within the overall §415(c) annual additions limit
A fixed 20% of net self-employment income with no deferral component
explanation: A Solo 401(k) allows two types of contributions: (1) employee elective deferral up to the standard 401(k) deferral limit, and (2) employer profit-sharing up to 25% of net self-employment income (after the SE tax deduction). The combined total is capped by the IRC §415(c) annual additions limit, which the IRS indexes each year. This can result in much higher contributions than a SEP-IRA for high-earning sole proprietors.

prompt: After the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, 529 plan funds can be used tax-free for which K-12 expenses?
choices: Any K-12 expense including extracurricular activities and transportation, up to $10,000/year
Only K-12 public school tuition, up to $10,000/year per student
K-12 tuition at public, private, or religious schools, up to $10,000/year per student
K-12 expenses are not covered by 529 plans — only higher education qualifies
explanation: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (2017) expanded 529 plans to cover K-12 tuition (public, private, or religious schools) up to $10,000/year per student. This applies at the federal level; some states don't conform and may treat K-12 529 withdrawals as non-qualified (subject to state income tax and penalty). Room & board and other expenses remain higher-education only.
source: IRS Pub 970; Tax Cuts and Jobs Act §11032

prompt: An HSA is often described as 'triple-tax-advantaged.' Which combination correctly identifies all three tax benefits?
choices: Deductible contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free qualified withdrawals
Deductible contributions, tax-deferred growth, tax-free withdrawals after age 65
After-tax contributions, tax-free growth, penalty-free withdrawals at any age
Employer-only contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free qualified withdrawals
explanation: An HSA's triple tax benefit: (1) Contributions are tax-deductible (or pre-tax via payroll), reducing taxable income; (2) investments inside the HSA grow tax-free; (3) withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are completely tax-free. After age 65 you can withdraw for any reason (taxed as ordinary income, like a Traditional IRA) — making an HSA an excellent long-term investment vehicle if you can pay medical costs out-of-pocket now.

prompt: What is Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA) and how does it help with employer stock in a 401(k)?
choices: NUA is the increase in an ETF's price above its NAV, taxed at ordinary income rates
NUA allows you to roll employer stock from a 401(k) to a brokerage, paying ordinary income tax only on the original cost basis — with the gain taxed at long-term capital gains rates
NUA is a deduction for appreciated stock donated to charity from a 401(k)
NUA is the market premium paid for a mutual fund above its book value
explanation: Net Unrealized Appreciation (NUA) is a tax strategy for employer stock inside a 401(k). Instead of rolling to an IRA (where all withdrawals are ordinary income), you take a lump-sum distribution: pay ordinary income tax on the cost basis only, then hold the shares in a taxable account. The appreciation (NUA) above the cost basis is taxed at long-term capital gains rates when sold — potentially saving significant taxes if the stock appreciated a lot.

prompt: What is the 'mega backdoor Roth' and which plan feature enables it?
choices: Converting a Traditional IRA to a Roth using a large one-time conversion in a low-income year

Making after-tax (non-Roth) 401(k) contributions beyond the standard deferral limit, then converting or rolling them to a Roth — enabled by an in-plan Roth conversion or in-service distribution feature
A strategy to avoid the pro-rata rule by first rolling all IRA balances into a 401(k)
Maximizing a backdoor Roth IRA by making two separate contributions in the same year
explanation: The mega backdoor Roth lets you contribute after-tax dollars to a 401(k) beyond the regular employee deferral limit — up to the total IRC §415(c) annual additions limit (minus employer contributions and your deferrals). The key requirement: your plan must allow after-tax contributions AND either in-plan Roth conversions or in-service withdrawals. Not all plans offer this. The IRS confirmed it's permissible in IRS Notice 2014-54.

prompt: What is the annual gift tax exclusion and what does exceeding it trigger?
choices: A per-recipient yearly limit; exceeding it requires immediate payment of gift tax
A per-recipient yearly limit (indexed for inflation); exceeding it requires filing Form 709 and reduces your lifetime exclusion
A per-recipient yearly limit applicable only to direct family members
A per-calendar-year total across all recipients combined before gift tax applies
explanation: The annual gift tax exclusion lets you give up to a per-recipient amount (indexed for inflation; a married couple can split gifts to double it) without gift tax consequences. Gifts above this amount require filing IRS Form 709 and reduce your lifetime estate and gift tax exemption rather than triggering an immediate tax for most people. Common planning use: front-loading a 529 with the 5-year election spreads five years of exclusions into one year per donor per beneficiary.
